title: C++ new and delete Operators
content:
### new and delete Operators

The `new` operator allocates memory on the heap and calls the constructor. The `delete` operator calls the destructor and deallocates memory.

---

### Using new

```cpp
int* p = new int;            // Default-initialized (uninitialized)
int* p2 = new int(42);       // Value-initialized to 42
int* p3 = new int();         // Value-initialized to 0

string* s = new string("Hello");  // Object with constructor args
```

---

### Using delete

```cpp
delete p;    // Single object
delete[] arr;  // Array
```

---

### new[] for Arrays

```cpp
int* arr = new int[10];       // 10 uninitialized ints
int* arr2 = new int[5]{1, 2, 3, 4, 5};  // Initialized

delete[] arr;   // Must use delete[] for arrays
delete[] arr2;
```

---

### Placement new

```cpp
#include <new>

char buffer[sizeof(int)];
int* p = new (buffer) int(42);  // Construct in existing memory
// No delete needed - just call destructor if needed
```

---

### new Failure (Nothrow)

```cpp
int* p = new (nothrow) int[1000000000];
if (p == nullptr) {
  cout << "Allocation failed";
}
```

---

### Overriding new/delete

```cpp
void* operator new(size_t size) {
  cout << "Custom alloc: " << size << " bytes";
  return malloc(size);
}

void operator delete(void* ptr) {
  cout << "Custom dealloc";
  free(ptr);
}
```

---

> **Key Point:** Always match `new` with `delete` and `new[]` with `delete[]`. Mixing them causes undefined behavior. In modern C++, prefer `std::make_unique` and `std::make_shared` over raw `new`.

quiz.question: Which delete operator is used for arrays allocated with new[]?
